request.question: Are Libocedrus and Helianthus both types of plants?

title: Sunflower (disambiguation)
Sunflower seed
Helianthus californicus
Helianthus eggertii
Helianthus
Libocedrus
Helianthus schweinitzii
Helianthus anomalus
List of Lepidoptera that feed on Helianthus
Alvordia
sentences: Sunflower ("Helianthus") is a genus of annual flowering plants native to North America.
The sunflower seed is the fruit of the sunflower ("Helianthus annuus").
There are three types of commonly used sunflower seeds: linoleic (most common), high oleic, and NuSun developed for sunflower oil.
Each variety has its own unique levels of monounsaturated, saturated, and polyunsaturated fats.
The information in this article refers mainly to the linoleic variety.
Helianthus californicus is a North American species of sunflower known by the common name California sunflower.
It is native to California in the United States and Baja California in Mexico, where it grows in many types of habitats.
Helianthus eggertii, known as Eggert's sunflower, is a North American species of flowering plants in the sunflower family.
It is native to Tennessee, Kentucky, South Carolina, and Alabama.
It is best known as one of the few plants to have been delisted under the Endangered Species Act because of the species' recovery.
It was described by John Kunkel Small in 1903.
Helianthus or sunflower (from the Greek: ήλιος , "Hēlios", "sun" and ανθός , "anthos", "flower") L. is a genus of plants comprising about 70 species in the family Asteraceae.
The genus is one of many in the Asteraceae that are known as sunflowers.
Except for three species in South America, all "Helianthus" species are native to North America.
The common name, "sunflower", typically refers to the popular annual species "Helianthus annuus", or the common sunflower, whose round flower heads in combination with the ligules look like the sun.
This and other species, notably Jerusalem artichoke ("H. tuberosus"), are cultivated in temperate regions and some tropical regions as food crops for humans, cattle, poultry and ornamental plants.
The largest sunflower field is located in Tuscany, Italy.
Libocedrus is a genus of five species of coniferous trees in the cypress family Cupressaceae, native to New Zealand and New Caledonia.

The genus is closely related to the South American genera "Pilgerodendron" and "Austrocedrus", and the New Guinean genus "Papuacedrus", both of which are included within "Libocedrus" by some botanists.
These genera are rather similar to the Northern Hemisphere genera "Calocedrus" and "Thuja": in earlier days, what is now "Calocedrus" was sometimes included in "Libocedrus".
They are much less closely related, as recently confirmed (Gadek et al. 2000).
The generic name means "teardrop cedar", apparently referring to drops of resin.
Helianthus schweinitzii, known by the common name Schweinitz's sunflower, is a perennial wildflower endemic to the Piedmont physiographic province of North Carolina and South Carolina.
It is classified in the Sunflower Family (Asteraceae).
Nationwide it is one of the rarest species of sunflower in the United States, but within its range it is not uncommon along utility and highway rights-of-way in North Carolina and South Carolina.
There are only about 90 known populations, many containing less than 40 plants each.
It was listed as a federal endangered species on May 7, 1991.
Helianthus anomalus (Western sunflower) is a species of plants in the sunflower family, found in the southwestern United States.
"Helianthus" species are used as food plants by the larvae of a number of Lepidoptera species including:
Alvordia is a genus of flowering plants in the family Asteraceae.
It includes 4 species of shrubs which occur in the states of Baja California and Sonora, Mexico.
The genus is characterized by having a secondary clustering of heads into compound units, so that what appears to be a single head is actually a group of heads packed together.
The genus is classified as a member of subtribe Helianthinae, the same subtribe that contains the common sunflower ("Helianthus").
Based on the reported chromosome counts, "Alvordia" includes both diploid and polyploid species, but the relationships among these have not yet been studied in detail.
No chromosome count has yet been reported for the species "A. congesta" from Sonora, which was a later transfer to the genus.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.

Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Are Libocedrus and Helianthus both types of plants?
hop1_docs: Libocedrus bidwillii | Libocedrus bidwillii variously called Pāhautea, Kaikawaka or New Zealand cedar' is a species of "Libocedrus", endemic to New Zealand on both the North and South Islands; on the North Island, it occurs from Te Aroha southward.  It grows at 250–1,200 m altitude in temperate rainforests.

Libocedrus | Libocedrus is a genus of five species of coniferous trees in the cypress family Cupressaceae, native to New Zealand and New Caledonia.  The genus is closely related to the South American genera "Pilgerodendron" and "Austrocedrus", and the New Guinean genus "Papuacedrus", both of which are included within "Libocedrus" by some botanists.  These genera are rather similar to the Northern Hemisphere genera "Calocedrus" and "Thuja": in earlier days, what is now "Calocedrus" was sometimes included in "Libocedrus".  They are much less closely related, as recently confirmed (Gadek et al. 2000).  The generic name means "teardrop cedar", apparently referring to drops of resin.

Helianthus annuus | Helianthus annuus, the common sunflower, is a large annual forb of the genus "Helianthus" grown as a crop for its edible oil and edible fruits (sunflower seeds).  This sunflower species is also used as bird food, as livestock forage (as a meal or a silage plant), and in some industrial applications.  The plant was first domesticated in the Americas.  Wild "Helianthus annuus" is a widely branched annual plant with many flower heads.  The domestic sunflower, however, often possesses only a single large inflorescence (flower head) atop an unbranched stem.  The name "sunflower" may derive from the flower's head's shape, which resembles the sun, or from the impression that the blooming plant appears to slowly turn its flower towards the sun as the latter moves across the sky on a daily basis.

Helianthus nuttallii subsp. nuttallii | H. nuttuallii subsp.  nuttallii, also known by the common name Nuttall's sunflower is a subspecies of "Helianthus nuttallii" in the genus "Helianthus" in the family Asteraceae.  It is considered by The Plant List to be a synonym for "H. nuttallii".  However, The Jepson Manual argues that it is distinct from the species.
Helianthus anomalus | Helianthus anomalus (Western sunflower) is a species of plants in the sunflower family, found in the southwestern United States.
List of Lepidoptera that feed on Helianthus | "Helianthus" species are used as food plants by the larvae of a number of Lepidoptera species including:
Libocedrus plumosa | Libocedrus plumosa, with the common name Kawaka, is a species of "Libocedrus" that is endemic to New Zealand.
hop1_titles: List of Lepidoptera that feed on Helianthus
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: Libocedrus (genus/species) and Helianthus (genus/species).

- Libocedrus: "Libocedrus is a genus of five species of coniferous trees in the cypress family Cupressaceae, native to New Zealand and New Caledonia." (passage 2)
- Libocedrus species example: "Libocedrus bidwillii ... is a species of 'Libocedrus', endemic to New Zealand" and "Libocedrus plumosa ... is a species of 'Libocedrus'." (passages 1,7)
- Helianthus: "'Helianthus annuus, the common sunflower, is a large annual forb of the genus \"Helianthus\"'." (passage 3)
- Helianthus species/family: "Helianthus anomalus ... is a species of plants in the sunflower family" and H. nuttallii is "in the genus 'Helianthus' in the family Asteraceae." (passages 5,4)

Ambiguity / missing facts: No ambiguity in the passages about whether these names refer to plants (genera/species); the passages explicitly state plant status. No additional fact is required to determine that both are types of plants.

Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
1. No additional retrieval needed (passages already confirm plant status).
2. If desired for verification or citations: fetch "Libocedrus" — lede/infobox and "Helianthus" — lede/infobox.
3. Optional deeper reads: "Libocedrus bidwillii" — article lede/"Distribution" and "Helianthus annuus" — article lede/"Description".

response.summary_2: Yes — both Libocedrus and Helianthus are types of plants. Libocedrus is a genus of five species of coniferous trees in the cypress family Cupressaceae native to New Zealand and New Caledonia. Helianthus includes the common sunflower (Helianthus annuus), an annual in the sunflower family Asteraceae.
